narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strength it takes to pursue your dreams in a field that needs more voices like yours. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.30 stands out as a solid achievement, slightly above the typical student's average, while your family comes from a middle-income background and you plan to attend full-time, just like most applicants. However, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 1 and Year 3 decile of 1—both well below the averages around the 5th or 6th decile—along with your LSAT score of 26, which is notably lower than the usual 37, painted a challenging picture for the admissions model. This model, trained on past students' data, predicted a 96.9% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is why your application was unfortunately rejected—law schools use this to prioritize those predicted to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking, as being in the bottom of your class that year strongly signaled potential struggles ahead. Your LSAT score was the next most influential, reinforcing concerns because lower scores like yours have historically correlated with bar exam difficulties for similar students. Your Year 1 decile also contributed notably in the same direction, highlighting early academic hurdles that the model weighed heavily. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA actually pulled in the opposite direction, suggesting strengths that could support passing, while your full-time status and family income had smaller positive leans compared to typical profiles. Overall, your situation differs from many admitted students who tend to rank higher in school deciles and score better on the LSAT, even if their other stats align with yours. This isn't the end of your path—consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, seeking targeted tutoring to improve class rankings in future studies, or exploring bridge programs and scholarships designed for underrepresented students like you to build a stronger bar exam foundation. You've already shown resilience with your GPA; channeling that into these steps could open new doors. We're rooting for you to rewrite this story.
